 INTELLIGENCE BRIEFING — As of 2025-01-25
 Subject: Donald J. Trump
 Knowledge Window: 2025-01-01 → 2025-01-25
 Events in window: 122 | Showing top 8
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

[2025-01-23] Senate Confirms John Ratcliffe as Trump's CIA Director
The U.S. Senate confirmed John Ratcliffe as the 25th Director of the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) on Thursday, January 23, 2025, with a 74-25 vote. Vice President JD Vance swore Ratcliffe into office approximately two hours later at the White House.

Ratcliffe previously served as Director of National Intelligence (DNI) during President Trump's first term, becoming the first individual to hold both the DNI and CIA Director positions. He also served as a U.S. congressman from Texas.

During his January 15, 2025, confirmation hearing, Ratcliffe outlined plans for the CIA to be more aggressive in collecting human intelligence and countering U.S. adversaries. Some Democrats, including Senators Ron Wyden and Chuck Schumer, opposed his nomination, citing concerns about partisanship, his experience, and his ability to challenge the administration.
Key facts:
  • 25th Director of the Central Intelligence Agency
  • Confirmation hearing held on January 15, 2025
  • First person to hold both CIA Director and DNI roles
  • First person to serve as both Director of the CIA and Director of National Intelligence
  • John Ratcliffe
  • John Ratcliffe confirmed as 25th CIA Director
  • John Ratcliffe confirmed as CIA Director
  • Pledged to reinvigorate Havana Syndrome investigations
  • Previously served as Director of National Intelligence (DNI)
  • Previously served as Director of National Intelligence during President Trump’s first administration
  • Previously served in the U.S. House of Representatives
  • Ratcliffe previously served as Director of National Intelligence
  • Senate Majority Leader John Thune advocated for speedy vote
  • Senate vote: 74-25
  • Senators Ron Wyden and Chuck Schumer opposed the nomination
  • Senators Tom Cotton and Mark Warner advocated for speedy vote
  • Supports maintaining Section 702 of FISA
  • Vice President JD Vance administered oath
  • Vice President JD Vance administered oath of office
"We will collect intelligence, especially human intelligence, in every corner of the globe no matter how dark or difficult." — John Ratcliffe (Confirmation hearing before the Senate Intelligence Committee)
"We will conduct covert actions at the direction of the president, going places no one else can go and doing things no one else can do." — John Ratcliffe (Confirmation hearing before the Senate Intelligence Committee)
"To the brave CIA officers listening around the world, if all of this sounds like what you signed up for, then buckle up and get ready to make a difference. If it doesn't, then it's time to find a new line of work." — John Ratcliffe (Confirmation hearing before the Senate Intelligence Committee)
"Our world is far too dangerous for any delay in having a Senate-confirmed leader in charge of the CIA." — Senators Tom Cotton and Mark Warner (Statement on Monday after committee advanced Ratcliffe's nomination)
"Under the Biden administration, the intelligence community made some notable misses. Mr. Ratcliffe brings the right experience and the right approach to the CIA." — John Thune (Senate floor, Tuesday, pushing for Ratcliffe's nomination)
"I want the record to show that I strongly oppose the nomination." — Ron Wyden (Senate floor, Tuesday)
"I will respectfully vote no, not because of our political difference, which of course exists, but because I am deeply worried that Mr. Ratcliffe will be unable to stand up to people like Donald Trump and Tulsi Gabbard, who are known to falsify intelligence." — Chuck Schumer (Senate floor, before Thursday's vote)
"it is the privilege of a lifetime to lead the patriotic men and women of the CIA, an agency so vital to America’s national security and one with a culture of unwavering commitment to mission. I’m extremely grateful for the opportunity to serve President Trump again and thank him for entrusting me with this tremendous responsibility." — Director Ratcliffe (Upon being sworn in as CIA Director)
Sources: www.npr.org, www.voanews.com, www.cia.gov (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-01-20] President Trump Nominates 47 Individuals for Key Sub-Cabinet and Agency Positions
On January 20, 2025, President Trump announced 47 nominations to the Senate for various sub-cabinet and agency positions. These included Director of the National Institutes of Health, Deputy Attorney General, Commissioner of Social Security, Administrator of NASA, Director of the Federal Bureau of Investigation, and Surgeon General. Nominations also covered Secretaries for the Army, Air Force, and Navy, and Deputy Secretaries for Defense, State, Treasury, Homeland Security, and Agriculture. Many nominees were slated to replace resigned officials, with the Director of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention being a new role. All nominations are subject to Senate confirmation.

Concurrently, the administration announced key cabinet appointments. Marco Rubio was confirmed as Secretary of State, Pete Hegseth as Defense Secretary, and Robert F Kennedy Jr as Health Secretary. Pam Bondi was named Attorney General. Elon Musk was appointed to lead a new Department of Government Efficiency (Doge) in an advisory capacity, tasked with downsizing the federal government. Other cabinet positions filled included Interior, Homeland Security, Transportation, Energy, Commerce, Education, Treasury, Labor, Housing, and Agriculture. Dr Marty Makary was nominated to lead the Food and Drug Administration.
Key facts:
  • 47 individuals nominated by President Trump to the Senate.
  • Brooke Rollins appointed Agriculture Secretary
  • Chris Wright appointed Energy Secretary
  • David Weldon's nomination for Director of CDC is for a new position.

  • Scott Bessent appointed Treasury Secretary
  • Scott Turner appointed Housing Secretary
  • Sean Duffy appointed Transportation Secretary
  • Susie Wiles appointed Chief of Staff
  • Tulsi Gabbard appointed Director of National Intelligence
  • William Long nominated for Commissioner of Internal Revenue for the remainder of the term expiring November 12, 2027.
"Nobody fights harder for the troops" — Donald Trump (Regarding Pete Hegseth's appointment as Defence Secretary)
"Pam was a prosecutor for nearly 20 years, tough on violent criminals, and made Florida's streets safe" — Donald Trump (Regarding Pam Bondi's appointment as Attorney General)
"course-correct and refocus the Agency" — Donald Trump (Regarding Dr Marty Makary's nomination to lead the Food and Drug Administration)

"new golden age with de-regulation, low-cost energy, [and] low taxes" — Scott Bessent (On the campaign trail, describing Trump's agenda)
"[He] has long been a strong advocate of the America First Agenda" — Donald Trump (Regarding Scott Bessent's appointment as Treasury Secretary)
"There is no climate crisis, and we're not in the midst of an energy transition either." — Chris Wright (In a video posted to his LinkedIn profile, prior to his appointment as Energy Secretary)
"dismantle government bureaucracy, slash excess regulations, cut wasteful expenditures and restructure federal agencies" — Donald Trump (Describing the purpose of the Department of Government Efficiency (Doge) led by Elon Musk)
Sources: www.whitehouse.gov, www.bbc.com (2 sources)

[2025-01-25] US Senate Confirms Kristi Noem as Secretary of Homeland Security
The U.S. Senate confirmed South Dakota Governor Kristi Noem as the 8th Secretary of Homeland Security on January 25, 2025, by a 59-34 vote. Noem, who previously served as South Dakota's 33rd Governor and its first female governor, resigned following her confirmation. Lt. Gov. Larry Rhoden succeeded her.

Noem is the fourth confirmed member of President Trump's cabinet, following Secretary of State Marco Rubio, CIA Director John Ratcliffe, and Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth. Her nomination advanced from the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee with a 13-2 vote.

As Secretary, Noem will oversee the Department of Homeland Security. She has expressed support for reinstating the 'Remain in Mexico' policy, shutting down the CPB One App, and ending birthright citizenship. Noem also identified cybersecurity, disaster relief, and counterterrorism as critical DHS missions. The department's responsibilities include border security measures, visa changes, and deportation efforts.

  • 8th Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security
  • Acting Secretary Benjamine Huffman

  • Senate vote: 59-34

  • Tom Homan picked as 'border czar'
"The reason that I asked for it is because I knew it was the president's number one priority." — Kristi Noem (Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee confirmation hearing)
"I knew that it would be needed to have someone in the position, that would do what the president promised the American people and would be strong enough to do it." — Kristi Noem (Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee confirmation hearing)
"We must be vigilant and proactive and innovative to protect the homeland. The challenges in front of us are extremely significant, and we must secure our borders against illegal trafficking and immigration. We must safeguard our critical infrastructure to make sure that we're protected against cyber attacks, respond to natural disasters and also terrorism." — Kristi Noem (Confirmation hearing)
"As a nation, we have the right and the responsibility to secure our borders against those who would do us harm. And we must create a fair and lawful immigration system that is efficient and is effective, and that reflects our values." — Kristi Noem (Confirmation hearing)
"work very well together and talk and communicate all the time. And we'll be working together on a daily basis when we're in our positions under the new administration. And I would say there's no authority being planned to be taken away from the department or myself if I'm in the role." — Kristi Noem (Confirmation hearing, regarding working with Tom Homan)
"As the Secretary of the Department of Homeland Security, I will work every day to keep all Americans safe and secure. One of my top priorities is achieving President Trump’s mandate from the American people to secure our southern border and fix our broken immigration system. The Trump Administration will once-again empower our brave men and women in law enforcement to do their jobs and remove criminal aliens and illegal gangs from our country. We will fully equip our intelligence and law enforcement to detect and prevent terror threats and will deliver rapid assistance and disaster relief to Americans in crisis. I thank President Trump and the US Senate for their trust in me. Together, we will ensure that the United States, once again, is a beacon of freedom, safety, and security for generations to come." — Kristi Noem (Statement on her confirmation as Secretary of Homeland Security)
Sources: www.npr.org, www.foxnews.com, www.dhs.gov (3 sources, verified date)

[2025-01-20] Donald Trump Signs Record 26 Executive Orders on First Day of Second Presidency
Donald Trump began his second term as President of the United States on January 20, 2025, signing 26 executive orders. On that day, he declared a "national energy emergency" and initiated the United States' withdrawal from the 2015 Paris Climate Agreement. He signed Executive Order 14154, "Unleashing American Energy," directing federal agencies to revise regulations on domestic energy resources and revoking the American Climate Corps.

Other initial actions included initiating withdrawal from the World Health Organization, revising federal recognition of gender identity, establishing the Department of Government Efficiency, pardoning over 1,500 individuals involved in the January 6 Capitol riot, designating Mexican drug cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, and declaring a national emergency at the southern border.

The administration also announced cabinet and cabinet-level nominations, including Marco Rubio for Secretary of State and Pamela Bondi for Attorney General. Individuals with backgrounds as oil, gas, and chemical lobbyists were appointed to the Environmental Protection Agency. Some of these orders have been blocked in court.
Key facts:

  • Analysis by Time and Bloomberg Government found nearly two-thirds of early executive actions mirrored Project 2025 proposals.
  • Attempted to end birthright citizenship.

[2025-01-20] President Reinstates and Amends Schedule F, Renaming it Schedule Policy/Career, and Revokes EO 14003
On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump signed multiple executive orders on his first day in office. These actions included declaring a national emergency at the Mexico–United States border, blocking asylum seekers, and ending the "catch and release" policy for undocumented individuals. Executive Order 14165 reinstated the Migrant Protection Protocols ("Remain in Mexico"), terminated the CBP One mobile application, and ended categorical parole programs. An executive order directed the end of birthright citizenship for children born on or after February 19, 2025, to parents without lawful permanent residence or U.S. citizenship. The measure is facing a lawsuit. Refugee admissions were suspended, effective January 27, 2025, and international cartels were designated as terrorists. Executive Order 14159, titled 'Protecting the American People Against Invasion,' revoked previous Biden administration immigration proclamations and mandated actions on interior enforcement and detention. Separately, an executive order reinstated and amended Executive Order 13957, renaming "Schedule F" to "Schedule Policy/Career" within the federal workforce. This order stated that employees must faithfully implement administration policies, with failure constituting grounds for dismissal, and revoked Executive Order 14003. The Director of the Office of Personnel Management was directed to amend Civil Service Regulations. The President also initiated a review of the USMCA trade agreement and other trade agreements.
Key facts:

  • "Schedule F" renamed "Schedule Policy/Career"

[2025-01-20] President Issues Memorandum Ordering Regulatory Freeze Pending Review for Executive Departments and Agencies
On January 20, 2025, President Trump issued a memorandum titled "Regulatory Freeze Pending Review" to the heads of executive departments and agencies. The directive ordered an immediate halt to proposing or issuing new rules without approval from department or agency heads appointed after January 20, 2025. Agencies were instructed to withdraw any rules sent to the Office of the Federal Register (OFR) but not yet published. The memorandum also directed agencies to consider postponing for 60 days the effective date of rules already published or issued but not yet in effect, to allow for review of factual, legal, and policy questions. The freeze applies to 'rules' as defined by the Administrative Procedure Act and Executive Order 12866. Exceptions are granted for emergency situations or rules subject to statutory or judicial deadlines, with the Director or Acting Director of the Office of Management and Budget authorized to grant such exemptions and oversee implementation. Similar regulatory freezes have been issued by previous administrations.
Key facts:
  • "Rule" definition includes regulatory actions (Executive Order 12866) and guidance documents (Executive Order 13891)
  • "Rules" defined under Administrative Procedure Act (5 U.S.C. § 551(4)) and Executive Order 12866

[2025-01-20] Trump Issues Executive Orders for Federal Employee Hiring Freeze and Return to In-Person Work
President Trump, following his inauguration on January 20, 2025, issued a series of executive actions. He directed the Secretary of State to align U.S. foreign policy with an "America First" directive, prioritizing American interests and citizens. On immigration, actions included "realigning" the Refugee Admission Program, designating cartels as foreign terrorist organizations, declaring a national emergency at the southern border, and directing construction of a border wall. The administration also moved to end "catch and release," reinstate "Remain in Mexico," and suspend refugee resettlement from January 27.

Regarding federal employees, Trump mandated all executive branch personnel return to in-person, full-time work, ending remote arrangements. He also imposed a 90-day hiring freeze on federal civilian employees, effective January 20, 2025, for unfilled positions, with exemptions for national security and immigration enforcement. The freeze excludes military personnel and prohibits using third-party contractors to bypass it. Additionally, Trump initiated actions to withdraw the U.S. from the Paris Agreement, declared a national energy emergency, and temporarily halted new wind energy projects on federal lands and waters. These orders also paused funding from the Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law.

"Our southern border is overrun by cartels, criminal gangs, known terrorists, human traffickers, smugglers, unvetted military-age males from foreign adversaries, and illicit narcotics that harm Americans" — Donald Trump (via signed order) (Order declaring a national emergency at the southern border)
"it is the policy of the United States to ensure that the Armed Forces of the United States prioritize the protection of the sovereignty and territorial integrity of the United States along our national borders." — Donald Trump (via signed order) (Order clarifying the military's role in border security)
"suspending the physical entry of aliens involved in an invasion into the United States across the southern border until I determine that the invasion has concluded" — Donald Trump (via proclamation) (Proclamation guaranteeing states protection against invasion)
"What this particular action does is it rescinds the open borders policies of the Biden administration and equips agents and officers of ICE and CBP with the authorities they need to deport people from the U.S. It also reaffirms the faithful execution of the immigration laws, prioritizes enforcement actions against criminal illegal aliens and establishes federal Homeland Security Task Forces to cooperate with state and local law enforcement in the removal of gangs, criminals and illegal aliens from the United States." — White House officials (Call with reporters previewing actions)
"So as you know, this is about national security, this is about public safety, and this is about the victims of some of the most violent, abusive criminals we've seen enter our country in our lifetime. And it ends today." — White House officials (Call with reporters previewing actions)
"We will drill, baby, drill" — Donald Trump (Inauguration speech on Jan. 20)
Sources: www.whitehouse.gov, www.npr.org, www.npr.org, www.foley.com (4 sources, verified date)
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
[2025-01-20] President Issues Memorandum Declaring OECD Global Tax Deal Has No Force or Effect in US
On January 20, 2025, President Donald Trump delivered his second inaugural address, outlining plans for executive orders related to deportations, energy, and tariffs.

On the same day, the President issued a memorandum stating that prior administration commitments regarding the Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) Global Tax Deal have no force or effect in the United States unless adopted by Congress. The memorandum directed the Treasury Secretary and the U.S. Permanent Representative to the OECD to formally notify the organization of this position. It also instructed the Treasury Secretary, in consultation with the United States Trade Representative, to investigate foreign countries for non-compliance with tax treaties or for implementing extraterritorial tax rules on American companies, requiring a list of responsive actions within 60 days.

Additionally, the administration issued Executive Order 14154, “Unleashing American Energy,” directing federal agencies to immediately pause the disbursement of funds appropriated through the Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 and the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. This has resulted in slowed funding for various programs and subsequent litigation.
Key facts:
  • 11% of Black voters for Trump (exit polls)
  • 46% of Latino voters for Trump (exit polls)
  • 60-day deadline for recommendations to the President
  • 62% disapproved of Trump pardoning Jan. 6 defendants (NPR poll)
  • Department of Energy (DOE) memorandum on January 23, 2025, suspended work on Community Benefits Plans and Justice40 requirements
  • Department of Transportation (DOT) suspended new obligations under National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure Formula Program on February 6, 2025
  • District Courts for the District of Columbia and Rhode Island enjoined implementation of the OMB memorandum
  • Donald Trump (President)
  • Elon Musk (attendee)
  • Emancipation Hall (location of informal speech)
  • Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) funding paused as of February 7, 2025
  • Executive Order (EO) 14154, "Unleashing American Energy"
  • Governor of Pennsylvania sued several agencies on February 13, 2025
  • Gulf of Mexico (to be renamed 'Gulf of America')
  • Hillary Clinton (former Secretary of State, reacted to Gulf of Mexico comment)
  • House Speaker Mike Johnson (present at informal speech)
  • Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 (IRA)
  • Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA)
  • Issued January 20, 2025
  • Jeff Bezos (attendee)
  • Joe Biden (former President)
  • Mark Zuckerberg (attendee)
  • Martin Luther King Jr. Day (date of inauguration)
  • OECD Global Tax Deal
  • OMB memorandum formally rescinded January 29, 2025
  • Office of Management and Budget (OMB) memorandum issued January 27, 2025
  • Permanent Representative of the United States to the OECD
  • Secretary of the Treasury
  • United States Trade Representative (USTR)
"The golden age of America begins right now. From this day forward, our country will flourish." — Donald Trump (Second inaugural address)
"We will be the envy of every nation and we will not allow ourselves to be taken advantage of any longer." — Donald Trump (Second inaugural address)
"We will do it at a level nobody has ever seen before." — Donald Trump (Second inaugural address, regarding deportations)
"In other words, you'll be able to buy the car of your choice." — Donald Trump (Second inaugural address, regarding electric vehicle mandates)
"My top priority will be to create a nation that is proud, prosperous and free." — Donald Trump (Second inaugural address)
"The scales of justice will be rebalanced. The vicious, violent and unfair weaponization of the Justice Department and our government will end." — Donald Trump (Second inaugural address)
"I was going to talk about the J6 hostages. But it's action, not words that count. And you're going to see a lot of action." — Donald Trump (Off-script speech in Emancipation Hall after formal address)
"By the way, that election was totally rigged. That was a rigged election. Had I thought we lost, I wouldn't run again … This time we made it too big to rig. They tried, they tried like hell. Then around 9:02, they gave up." — Donald Trump (Off-script speech to supporters)
"We have a government that has given unlimited funding to the defense of foreign borders, but refuses to defend American borders or, more importantly, its own people." — Donald Trump (Second inaugural address)
"To the Black and Hispanic communities, thank you for the trust and love you gave to me. We set records, and I will not forget it." — Donald Trump (Second inaugural address)
"We will strive to make his dream a reality. We will make his dream come true." — Donald Trump (Second inaugural address, referring to Martin Luther King Jr.'s dream)
"It is my hope that our recent presidential election will be remembered as the greatest and most consequential election in the history of our country." — Donald Trump (Second inaugural address)
